prompt: Consider the set of integers {1, 2, 3, ..., 25}. Count the number of 5-element subsets you can choose such that every pair of chosen numbers differs by at least 3 (i.e. no two selected numbers are within 2 of each other). Reason step by step: set up a gap/spacing model or a bijection to an unconstrained selection, derive the count, and compute it exactly. Give the final count as a single integer on the last line in the form \boxed{X}.

grading._solution: Pick 5 values a1<a2<...<a5 from 1..25 with a(i+1)-a(i) >= 3. Substitute b(i) = a(i) - 2*(i-1). Then the b(i) are strictly increasing (each gap reduced by 2, so gaps become >=1) and lie in 1..25-2*4 = 1..17. The map is a bijection between valid subsets and arbitrary 5-element subsets of {1..17}. Count = C(17,5) = 6188. Brute-force enumeration over all C(25,5)=53130 subsets confirms 6188.
